stem: Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to evaluate \( \int_{[[lo]]}^{[[hi]]} \left( ([[a]])x^{3} + ([[b]])x^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]]) \right)\, dx \).
solution: An antiderivative is \( F(x) = \frac{[[a]]}{4}x^{4} + \frac{[[b]]}{3}x^{3} + \frac{[[c]]}{2}x^{2} + ([[d]])x \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus the integral equals \( F([[hi]]) - F([[lo]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates the antiderivative only at the upper limit and forgets to subtract its value at the lower limit.
This subtracts the integrand's values \( f([[hi]]) - f([[lo]]) \) instead of antidifferentiating first.
This applies the power rule without dividing each term by its new exponent.
This is off by the width of the interval.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the standard antiderivatives of sine and cosine to evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[k]]\pi/2} \left( ([[a]])\sin x + ([[b]])\cos x \right) dx \).
solution: An antiderivative is \( F(x) = -([[a]])\cos x + ([[b]])\sin x \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus the integral equals \( F([[k]]\pi/2) - F(0) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the sign flip on the antiderivative of cosine (uses \( +a\cos x \) instead of \( -a\cos x \)).
This forgets to subtract \( F(0) = -([[a]]) \), i.e. omits the \( +[[a]] \) term.
This mixes up which coefficient multiplies sine versus cosine in the antiderivative.
This uses \( \sin x \) as the antiderivative of sine instead of \( -\cos x \), then still subtracts the wrong constant.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the standard antiderivative of an exponential to evaluate \( \int_{\ln [[p]]}^{\ln [[q]]} e^{[[n]] x}\, dx \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by the coefficient \( [[n]] \) from the antiderivative \( \tfrac{1}{[[n]]}e^{[[n]]x} \).
This divides by \( [[q]] \) instead of by \( [[n]] \).
This treats the exponent as if the integrand were linear, using \( q - p \) instead of \( q^{[[n]]} - p^{[[n]]} \).
This applies the power rule to the exponent, using \( [[n]]+1 \) as if this were \( x^{[[n]]} \) rather than \( e^{[[n]]x} \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the standard antiderivative \( \int \frac{1}{x+c}\,dx = \ln|x+c| \) to evaluate \( \int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} \frac{1}{x + ([[c]])}\, dx \).
solution: An antiderivative is \( F(x) = \ln|x + ([[c]])| \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus the integral equals \( F([[b]]) - F([[a]]) = \ln\!\left( \frac{[[b]] + ([[c]])}{[[a]] + ([[c]])} \right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two log values instead of subtracting them.
This takes the log of the interval width instead of the ratio of shifted endpoints.
This mistakes \( 1/(x+c) \) for having antiderivative \( -1/(x+c) \), as if it were a power rule term.
This forgets to shift by \( c \) before taking the logarithm of each endpoint.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the standard antiderivative \( \int \sec^{2}(u)\,du = \tan(u) \) to evaluate \( \int_{0}^{\pi/(4\cdot [[k]])} ([[a]])\sec^{2}([[k]]x)\, dx \).
solution: With \( u = [[k]]x \), an antiderivative of \( ([[a]])\sec^{2}([[k]]x) \) is \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[k]]}\tan([[k]]x) \). At \( x = \pi/(4[[k]]) \), \( [[k]]x = \pi/4 \) so \( \tan([[k]]x) = 1 \), giving \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[k]]}\left(1 - 0\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing by it when undoing the chain rule.
This divides by \( [[k]]^{2} \) instead of \( [[k]] \).
This treats the bound \( \pi/(4[[k]]) \) as the value of \( \tan \) itself rather than evaluating \( \tan(\pi/4) = 1 \).
This uses the bound value \( \pi/4 \) directly as \( \tan([[k]]x) \) and forgets to divide by \( [[k]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the standard antiderivative \( \int \frac{du}{1+u^{2}} = \arctan(u) \) to evaluate \( \int_{0}^{1/[[k]]} \frac{[[a]]}{1 + ([[k]]x)^{2}}\, dx \).
solution: With \( u = [[k]]x \), an antiderivative of \( \frac{[[a]]}{1+([[k]]x)^{2}} \) is \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[k]]}\arctan([[k]]x) \). At \( x = 1/[[k]] \), \( [[k]]x = 1 \) so \( \arctan([[k]]x) = \pi/4 \), giving \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[k]]}\cdot\frac{\pi}{4} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing by it when undoing the chain rule.
This uses \( \arctan(1) = \pi/2 \) instead of the correct \( \pi/4 \).
This drops the \( \pi/4 \) factor from \( \arctan(1) \) entirely.
This forgets to divide by \( [[k]] \) after undoing the chain rule.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the standard antiderivative \( \int \frac{du}{\sqrt{1-u^{2}}} = \arcsin(u) \) to evaluate \( \int_{0}^{1/[[k]]} \frac{[[a]]}{\sqrt{1 - ([[k]]x)^{2}}}\, dx \).
solution: With \( u = [[k]]x \), an antiderivative of \( \frac{[[a]]}{\sqrt{1-([[k]]x)^{2}}} \) is \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[k]]}\arcsin([[k]]x) \). At \( x = 1/[[k]] \), \( [[k]]x = 1 \) so \( \arcsin([[k]]x) = \pi/2 \), giving \( \frac{[[a]]}{[[k]]}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( [[k]] \) instead of dividing by it when undoing the chain rule.
This uses \( \pi/4 \) instead of the correct \( \arcsin(1) = \pi/2 \).
This drops the \( \pi/2 \) factor from \( \arcsin(1) \) entirely.
This uses \( \pi/4 \) and also forgets to divide by \( [[k]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Use the power rule \( \int x^{-2}\,dx = -x^{-1} \) to evaluate \( \int_{[[p]]}^{[[q]]} \frac{[[a]]}{x^{2}}\, dx \).
solution: An antiderivative of \( \frac{[[a]]}{x^{2}} \) is \( -\frac{[[a]]}{x} \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus the integral equals \( -\frac{[[a]]}{[[q]]} - \left(-\frac{[[a]]}{[[p]]}\right) = \frac{[[a]]}{[[p]]} - \frac{[[a]]}{[[q]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the sign flip from the negative exponent, using \( a/x \) as the antiderivative instead of \( -a/x \).
This applies the power rule as if the exponent were \( -3 \) rather than \( -2 \).
This treats the integrand as constant equal to \( a \) times the interval width.
This divides by the interval width instead of antidifferentiating.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
